advantages: Reduces parameters from exponential in feature count to linear: estimate only P(xᵢ|y) per (feature, class) pair instead of the full joint P(x|y).
Training and prediction are fast—often O(nd) for n samples and d features—with closed-form or count-based estimates.
Works well with high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., bag-of-words) where joint estimation would be impossible.
Provides interpretable per-feature contributions via log P(xᵢ|y), useful for debugging and explaining predictions.
Combines naturally with Bayes' theorem for principled class posteriors and handles multiple classes via the same product formula.
disadvantages: Biased likelihood when features are strongly correlated given y—the product can over- or under-estimate P(x|y) and miscalibrate P(y|x).
Cannot represent feature interactions (e.g., bigrams, negation) without engineering composite features or switching models.
Zero-frequency problem: unseen (xᵢ, y) pairs assign zero probability to entire classes unless smoothing is applied.
Posterior probabilities can be poorly calibrated even when argmax classification accuracy is good—probability outputs should not be trusted blindly.
Choosing wrong per-feature distribution (Gaussian on categorical data, etc.) violates modeling assumptions and degrades performance.
